content.full_text:
The GOVERNMENT
TECHNOLOGY GROUP, Inc.
.joining government and technology to better serve the people

Nov 14 10 05 AM '91


November 11, 1991


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

We are in receipt of your advice letter to Steve Kolodney dated October
29, 1991.

Enclosed is a copy of a letter which we sent to Mr. Kolodney in an effort
to establish a procedure by which we could keep him informed of those
areas of interest to The Government Technology Group, and through
which we could avoid conflicts.

We note from our telephone conversation and from your letter to Mr.
Kolodney that he sent a letter to you in regard to this matter. It is our
understanding that we were shown as having received a copy of that
letter. However, our records indicate we have not received that copy and
we would appreciate it if you would forward a copy to us.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Michael Franchetti
Chairman of the Board


mvf/lp
enclosure
cc: David R. Lema


2700 Gateway Oaks Drive, Suite 240
Sacramento, CA 95833
916-641-1600 FAX 916-641-5706

The GOVERNMENT
TECHNOLOGY GROUP, Inc.

...joining government and technology to better serve the people

November 11, 1991


Mr. Steven Kolodney, Director
Office of Information Technology
915 L Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Steve,

I am contacting you in my capacity as Chairman of the Board of The
Government Technology Group to follow-up on the October 29, 1991, letter
to you from the California Fair Political Practices Commission. As you
know, The Government Technology Group contacted the Fair Political
Practices Commission to seek guidance on the question of whether or not
you had a conflict of interest based upon your ownership and Diedre
Kolodney's ownership of shares in The Government Technology Group. It is
our understanding that you followed-up on our request with a request of
your own, and that the Fair Political Practices Commission letter of
October 29, 1991, was in response to your request.

The October 29, 1991, letter advises you that you may not take part in
a decision which "will foreseeably have a material financial effect on
The Government Technology Group, Inc....". The letter further advises you
that whether or not the financial consequences of the decision are
"reasonably foreseeable" depends upon the facts of the case. It further
states that "an effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur."

The Board is very concerned that your stock ownership could cause
substantial embarrassment to both The Government Technology Group and to
you, and could create legal complications for The Government Technology
Group, its clients, and you should you make a decision which would be in
violation of the law. In order to do as much as possible to avoid this
unwanted possibility, the Board has decided that The Government Technology
Group will do all it can to keep you apprised of areas in which The
Government Technology Group is working on its own behalf or on behalf of
clients so that you can make an informed decision as to whether or not you
should participate in activities pertaining to those areas. A list of
current subject areas and current clients is enclosed for your
information, and we will continue to update you on a regular basis as the
areas and clients change and expand.


2700 Gateway Oaks Drive, Suite 240
Sacramento, CA 95833
916-641-1600   FAX 916-641-5706

Mr. Steven Kolodney
November 11,1991
Page 2

Although at any one point in time The Government Technology Group will
have a defined list of areas of interest and of clients, the Board is
concerned over the fact that in reality The Government Technology Group is
involved in or intends to be involved in every major issue pertaining to
information technology in California. This involvement might be directly
on behalf of a current client, or it might be on behalf of The Government
Technology Group itself in an effort to influence state policy in a manner
which will lead to activity by The Government Technology Group on behalf
of current or potential clients.

In view of the wide range of activities in which the Office of
Information Technology engages and your decision-making responsibilities
in terms of feasibility report approval, etc., you and GIG need to work
out a system of exchanging information so that you are aware of what
matters are before you which will create a conflict situation as defined
in the October 29, 1991, FPPC letter. Toward this end, we would suggest a
meeting within the next two nweeks to enable us to begin the process of
exchanging the necessary information and to set up further necessary
procedures to avoid any conflict of interest issues which would be harmful
to you as well as GIG and its clients.

Dave Lema, President and Chief Executive Officer of The Government
Technology Group will contact you in the near future to arrange a
convenient time for you and The Government Technology Group to meet to
discuss and resolve these potential conflict problems.

It is the hope of the Board of The Government Technology Group that by
keeping each other fully informed we will be able to avoid any conflicts
or other questionable conduct arising from your ownership of Government
Technology Group stock.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
MICHAEL FRANCHETTI

MVF:jm

cc: Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
Dave Lema

Steve E. Kolodney- PPPC

October 15, 1991                                OCT 16 12:48 PM '91

Mr. John Wallace
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Wallace:

Thank you for your telephone call and for the opportunity to provide you with additional information.

I believe that the facts regarding my stock ownership in The Government Technology Group, Inc. (GTG) and the duties of the Director of the Office of Information Technology which were presented in Mr. Franchetti's letter of September 20,1991, are substantially correct when considered in conjunction with the information in my letter of September 24, 1991. Because I left GTG more than a year ago and have not been involved with the company since then, I have no current knowledge of GTG's business plan or activities.

While these facts are substantially correct, I do not agree with the conclusions that Mr. Franchetti reaches or the inferences he draws about the potential for conflict of interest. My principle responsibility is to review and approve project proposals from state agencies for the use of information technology. Once approved, many of these projects are carried out within the existing resources of the agencies. Others are accomplished using the technologies of the state's data centers.

When it is necessary for an agency to acquire information technology from the private sector, the procurement is conducted by the Office of Procurement, Department of General Services, or by the agency itself, under delegation authority from the Department of General Services. Such a procurement would occur only after the project proposal has been approved.

Technology procurements are usually competitive bids in the form of requests for proposal or price quotation. Specifications are developed by and bids are evaluated by the requesting agency and bids are evaluated by impartial evaluation committees established by that agency. I do not participate on these evaluation committees or review their determinations.

I would also like to reconfirm my request for an advice letter so that I can act within the rules, even as I actively continue to liquidate my stock ownership in GTG. I understand that my request and your advice to me are public information.

Once again, thank you for your assistance in this matter. If I can provide you with any additional information, please let me know.

Sincerely,

Steve E. Kolodney

                                                    7636 River Ranch Way
                                                    Sacramento, CA 95831
                                                    916-391-8980

Steve E. Kolodney

September 24, 1991

Mr. Ben Davidian
Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Davidian:

Mr. Michael Franchetti, Chairman of the Board of The Government Technology Group, Inc. (GTG) has provided me with a copy of the letter he sent to you on September 20, 1991, in which he raised certain questions about my ownership of GTG stock.

For the past year, I have actively attempted to liquidate my interest in GTG. Until September 13, 1991, when GTG abruptly revoked their settlement offer that I had accepted, GTG and I were moving toward resolution of our business interests. That settlement would have returned all shares of GTG common stock owned by me and my wife, Deidre, to the company. These negotiations have apparently broken down and there is no market for GTG shares.

I am not an officer, or an employee of GTG and I do not actively participate in any activity involving the company. I have disclosed my ownership of stock in GTG and I have disqualified myself in writing from the one situation in which there might have been even the slightest perception of a conflict of interest. I am prepared to disqualify myself in the unlikely event that my office is asked to participate in any other decision which might beneficially effect GTG.

I do not believe that a conflict of interest would arise from my duties reviewing projects from state departments because these proposals do not specify any vendor's products and because their approval precedes any procurement activity. Furthermore, my office is not responsible for either acquiring technology or selecting contractors. However, since I wish to avoid even the appearance of a conflict, I, too, would appreciate your guidance on this matter.

I would be happy to provide you with any additional information which might be helpful.

Thank you for your assistance.

Sincerely,

Steve E. Kolodney

7636 River Ranch Way
Sacramento, CA 95831
916-391-8980

The GOVERNMENT
TECHNOLOGY GROUP, Inc.
...joining government and technology to better serve the people

FPPC

OCT 31 12:17PM'91

October 29, 1991

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Hallabrin,

This is to follow-up on our recent conversation concerning our letter
of September 9, 1991, asking for advice concerning the impact of stock
ownership in the Government Technology Group by Steve Kolodney the
Director of the Office of Information Technology.

Our request that the information be kept confidential is based on our
desire to avoid any potential embarrassment to Mr. Kolodney, and to keep
as low a profile in regard to this matter as possible. However, in view
of both your policy and Mr. Kolodney's letter requesting that the advice
be given, we waive the confidentiality request.

We have enclosed a copy of a document prepared by the Office of
Information Technology which outlines the scope and role of the office as
it is currently conceived. You will note that the office not only is
involved in direct review of feasibility studies that have already been
prepared, but also indicates that its intent is to work with departments
and agencies in developing, from the very beginning, policies and
proposals involving information technology. Our concern over this matter
arises from both the situation in which the Office of Information
Technology would directly review a feasibility study or a proposed
contract, and the situation in which the office would work with decision
makers in departments and agencies to develop programs which would
ultimately result in the issuance of contracts.

In the former case our concern would be whether or not the direct
involvement of the office or Mr. Kolodney in making a decision pertaining
to a specific feasibility study or contract would raise a question of
conflict of interest. In the latter case our concern is whether or not
the formulation of a policy or the development of a concept which would
ultimately lead to a contract is an activity of such a nature as to raise

2700 Gateway Oaks Drive, Suite 240
Sacramento, CA 95833
916-641-1600 FAX 916-641-5706

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
October 29, 1991
Page 2

questions as to whether or not an individual might, in theory, slant the
policy or concept to favor a client or a firm in which the individual has
a substantial ownership interest.

You informed us that the letter that Mr. Kolodney sent to you showed a
carbon copy to me. We have not received our copy, and would appreciate it
if you would forward a copy to us. This would be helpful because there
may be statements or comments in the letter which we would like to
elaborate on or further discuss to assist you in best evaluating the
situation.

Very truly yours,

[Signature]
MICHAEL FRANCHETTI

MVF:jm
Enclosure

CC: Steve Kolodney

September 20, 1991


Mr. Ben Davidian
Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California  95814

Dear Ben,

I am contacting you as Chairman of the Board of the Government Technology Group, Inc. The Government Technology Group is a California S Corporation which was incorporated in the Fall of 1989.

We are concerned that ownership shares in the corporation by a state official may give rise to conflicts on that individuals part; and request a review of the situation by your staff, and guidance from you as to whether or not this share ownership is incompatible with the individuals current state position.

The Government Technology Group has six shareholders. 12 1/2 percent of the shares are owned by Steven Kolodney, who is currently the Director of the Office of Information Technology. An additional 12 1/2 percent of the shares are owned by his wife, Diedre Kolodney.

When Mr. Kolodney and his wife initially obtained these shares, he was an employee of The Government Technology Group, and was not in state service. However, he left The Government Technology Group in the Fall of 1990 and in early 1991 was appointed to the position of Director of the Office of Information Technology. This is a position which he had held prior to leaving state service in 1989 in order to become an employee and shareholder of The Government Technology Group.

The Government Technology Group specializes in working with private sector and government clients who have an interest in the information technology field. Most of the private sector clients of The Government Technology Group are interested in obtaining contracts with state and local governments, including the State of California, for the provision of information technology services, software, or hardware. The Government Technology Group assists these clients in various ways including providing them with the help needed to develop a strategy for obtaining state business, keeping them apprised of current state policies and developments which may provide business opportunities, assisting them in preparing responses to requests for proposals from state agencies, assisting them in post-award proceedings, and assisting them in carrying out state contracts once they have been issued.

Because of its expertise and knowledge, The Government Technology Group also is looked to by state and local government officials as a resource for information pertaining to what technologies are available in the private sector, and as a resource for

2700 Gateway Oaks Drive, Suite 240
Sacramento, CA 95833
916-641-1600    FAX 916-641-5706

[The image appears to be mostly blank or extremely faded, with only very faint traces of text that are not legible enough to transcribe accurately. The visible elements are too unclear to read with confidence.]

Mr. Ben Davidian
September 20, 1990
Page 2

discussing long-term strategies and plans for expanding information technology usage at
the state and local government level.

The Government Technology Group does not engage in activities which would be defined
as lobbying. However, it has retained a contract lobbying firm to represent it on policy
issues when it believes that such representation would be helpful to the general
development of information technology in California.

The Government Technology Group is paid in a variety of ways. In some cases it receives
a fixed fee for services rendered. In other cases it receives a fee which is keyed to the
successs of the private sector vendor in that it receives a percentage of the contract
actually awarded to the vendor and acts as a subcontractor to the vendor in right-of-way
implementation of a contract. Generally, government entities pay the Government
Technology Group on a fixed-fee or hourly fee basis.

As shareholders of The Government Technology Goup both Mr. Kolodney and his wife
directly share in any profits generated by the company. Because this is a S
corporation, profits are directly divided among shareholders, as losses.

It is our understanding that as Director of the Office of Information Technology Mr.
Kolodney plays a role in establishing state information technology policy and strategy,
and is responsible for approving feasibility reviews of proposed information
technology projects which are submitted to his office by various state agencies. if
approved, these feasibility studies become the basis for proposed contracts which would
be potentially bid upon by clients of The Government Technology Group. In addition, it
is our understanding that Mr. Kolodney's role is to advise the Department of Finance as
to the feasibility of funding various projects, and has in the past reviewed the feasibility
of funding information technology projects which had exceeded the hurds budgeted for
them. in such a case, of course, his recommendation would have major impact upon the
State's decision to accept or reject a proposed contract.

The Board of The Government Technology Group is concerned that Mr. Kolodney's
current role with state government and his (and possibly his wife's) continued ownership
of stock in The Government Technology Group is incompatible. The Board is concerned
that contracts which are awarded to clients of The Government Technology Group might
be subject to protest on the basis that Mr. Kolodney's involvement in the process tainted
the process, and that decisions that he or his office made were designed to further his
personal interests through benefiting Government Technology Group clients.

As a practical matter, you should be advised that Mr. Kolodney left The Government
Technology Group under hostile circumstances, and that the Board of Directors views his
current ownership of shares as hostile to the corporation. Be that as it may, Mr.
Kolodney and his wife have a direct financial interest in the success of The Government
Technology Group, and will directly share in any profits which are generated by The
Government Technology Group's activities.

Our question to the Commission is:

1) Given the above information, is Mr. Kolodney's continued ownership of shares in
The Government Technology Group compatible with his position as Director of
the Office of Information Technology, or does it create a conflict which needs to
be resolved in some manner;

Mr. Ben Davidian
September 20, 1991
Page 3

2)     Is Diedre Kolodney's continued ownership of shares in The Government
       Technology Group of such as nature as to be covered by the answer given to
       question #1?

3)     If there are conflicts created by Mr. Kolodney's or Mrs. Kolodney's ownership of
       Government Technology Group shares, what can The Government Technology
       Group do to ensure that its clients are not harmed by this situation.

We are prepared to provide you with such additional information as you require, and are
hopeful that you can provide us with some guidance in regard to this difficult situation.

It is my understanding that your response to this letter will be confidential. Thank you
in advance for your consideration of our questions.

Very Truly yours,


MICHAEL FRANCHETTI

[signature]

mvf/jl
cc: Steven Kolodney


[Yellow sticky note with handwritten text:]
Greg-D gave him a copy
of this letter. She said
technical questions addressed
to Jim should be forwarded
to you.
